label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about inferring the meaning of the headline "Safina goes from basket case to top of the world." The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to understand what each part of the headline implies. "Basket case" usually refers to someone who is emotionally unstable or in a bad state. The article mentions that Safina is emotionally explosive, like her brother, and a newspaper called her "mad as a snake." So "basket case" here likely refers to her emotional volatility or nervousness during her matches. The second part of the headline is "top of the world," which means she became the world number one. The article states she's moving up to be the top ranked player. So the headline is contrasting her previous emotional struggles (basket case) with her current success (top of the world). The question is what this implies. Looking at the options: A) She wanted to top the world as a baby in a basket. That doesn't make much sense. A baby in a basket isn't related to the phrase "basket case." Probably not A. B) After winning the Australian Open, she cried. The headline doesn't mention crying. It's more about emotional state to success. So maybe not B. C) Too nervous at first but gained confidence and won. This fits with the idea of being a basket case (nervous/emotionally unstable) and then becoming top. The article says she has made it to finals and won a silver medal, showing improvement. So C could be right. D) Learnt from the basket case. The headline doesn't mention learning from someone else called a basket case. It's more about her own journey from being unstable to successful. So
